query: latest WHO recommendations for infant immunization schedule

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- Annual Updates in the CDC Immunization Schedule
retrieved_docs.content: CDC issues annual updates in immunization schedules. [7] New vaccines were added in some of the annual updates, eg, Chickenpox (1996), hepatitis A (2000), pneumococcal vaccine (2001). Few vaccines were discontinued from the schedule, eg, the oral polio vaccine (2004). New versions of existing vaccines were added, eg, DTaP (1997), intranasal influenza (2004). Additional recommendations were advised for already existing vaccines. The current immunization schedules for children below 6 years comprises vaccines that prevent 14 infectious diseases, which is a drastic achievement compared with the schedule in 1948 when immunizations against only diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, and smallpox were available. Per the immunization schedule, a child may receive up to 24 injections in the first 2 years of age and up to 5 injections in a single visit.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: Catch-up vaccination for Haemophilus influenza is not recommended for previously unvaccinated children above 5 years of age without risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: If a DTaP or Tdap vaccine is given at 10 years of age for other reasons, it can be included as the booster dose in the CDC schedule. If the DTaP or Tdap vaccine is given at 7 to 9 years of age, we should not include it as the booster dose, and Tdap should be readministered at eleven to twelve years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: The 5th dose of the DTaP vaccine is unnecessary if the fourth dose was given after 4 years of age and 6 months after the third dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: The catch-up immunization for Hepatitis A vaccine is recommended for all unvaccinated individuals aged 2 to 18.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: Catch-up vaccination for diphtheria or tetanus after the first dose of Tdap can be given as Tdap or Td. Tdap can be used wherever only Td vaccine is indicated, eg, tetanus prophylaxis after wound management.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: The age group 19 to 21 years and 22 to 26 years are combined as 19 to 26 years in the 2020 CDC schedule due to the change in the recommendation of catch-up HPV vaccination for all individuals aged up to 26.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: CDC 2020 recommends booster doses for Meningococcal B for persons above 10 years with risk factors like asplenia, complement deficiency, and microbiologists.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV) doses may be counted toward the United States vaccination requirements. The OPV administered before April 1, 2016, should be counted.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization is the most effective and safe public health intervention in reducing infectious disease incidence, prevalence, morbidity, and mortality. [1] [2] [3] Vaccination is the most cost-effective preventive service. Despite the advancement in vaccine coverage, more than 40,000 adults and 300 children die every year in the USA from vaccine-preventable diseases. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Influenza Vaccination
retrieved_docs.content: The minimum age for the influenza vaccine is 6 months for the inactivated vaccine and 2 years for the live vaccine. For the age group 6 months to 8 years, 2 doses 4 weeks apart are recommended for those getting them for the first time. In subsequent years, kids up to 8 years can get only 1 dose annually. One dose is recommended for all individuals above 9 years of age. Individuals with egg allergies can get the influenza vaccine under medical supervision. Measles, Mumps, and Rubella Vaccination (MMR)

retrieved_docs.content: For high-risk children of age group 2 to 5 years, a 2-dose series of PCV13 with 8 weeks intervals followed by 1 dose of PPSV23 at least 8 weeks after the last PCV13 dose.

retrieved_docs.content: Meningococcal B vaccination for 16 to 23 years HPV vaccine for 27 to 45 years PCV13 is for elderly individuals over 65 years old without risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a broad category that includes several specific maternal health issues. It has the child condition قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (light vomiting of pregnancy) and is classified as encompassing الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery), الحصبة الألمانية خلال فترة الحمل (German measles during pregnancy), and خلل وظيفة الدرقية في فترة الحمل (thyroid dysfunction in the gestational period). These various complications highlight the diverse risks that can affect pregnant individuals and underscore the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and appropriate management to protect both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures comprise a range of interventions used to manage complex obstetric conditions and facilitate safe delivery. Among these, the surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancy and abdominal pregnancy represents critical operative approaches for addressing abnormal implantation of the embryo, thereby preventing life‑threatening complications for the mother. In addition, Vaginal Assisted Delivery is included as a procedural option that supports the progression of labor and assists in the birth of the infant when additional support is required. Together, these child procedures under the umbrella of Pregnancy-Related Procedures illustrate the spectrum of surgical and assisted techniques employed in modern obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures is a broad category of obstetric interventions that encompasses specific techniques such as بضع الفرج and Vaginal Assisted Delivery, each classified as a child procedure within this group. Both بضع الفرج and Vaginal Assisted Delivery represent assisted vaginal delivery methods used to facilitate safe childbirth when spontaneous vaginal delivery is not optimal, highlighting the importance of tailored procedural options in maternal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2347
retrieved_docs.content: In obstetric practice, the overarching topic “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery” includes the specific condition “Malposition; malpresentation,” which is classified as a subcategory (has parent) within these care indications. This relationship highlights that malposition and malpresentation are considered part of the broader set of indications guiding clinical management during pregnancy, labor, and delivery.
